input: I pick up a large rock and hurl it into the black lake at the edge of the Woods of Unmake.
output: The rock leaves your hand with a grunt of effort and arcs through the rust-colored air before striking the lake's surface. But the sound it makes is wrong — not the clean crack of displaced water you expect, but a low, wet thud, as if the lake has no intention of being disturbed. For a heartbeat, nothing. Then the ripples spread — but they move inward, not outward, pulling toward the point of impact rather than away from it. The surface tension seems to resist the disturbance, reluctant to accept that something has entered it. Two herons that were standing motionless at the far bank launch themselves skyward in a burst of grey wings, their cries sharp and afraid. They do not fly into the tree line. They fly up, and keep going, as if they want no part of what is below them. The ripples reach the shore at your feet. The water that laps against the mud is slightly darker than the water a meter further out — and cold. Not cold like winter. Cold like the moment before you understand something that frightens you.

input: I light a torch and hold it up in the middle of the Woods of Unmake.
output: The flame catches with a familiar hiss, and for a moment everything is almost ordinary — the amber warmth pushes back the shadows around you and you can see the gnarled, geometrically impossible trees in detail: bark that spirals the wrong way, branches that fork at right angles, roots that rise from the earth and re-enter it in loops. The light is good. But the forest notices it. First, the insect sounds — the clicks and low hums you had barely registered — stop. A silence plugs the air like cotton in a wound. Then, from somewhere above and to your left, something shifts its weight on a branch. Something large. It does not come down. It just moves — repositioning, you think, to stay at the edge of the light. Whatever it is, it does not want to be in the circle you have made, but it is not leaving either. Forty meters ahead, at the boundary of your torchlight, you can see the pale shapes of three Hollowmen. They are motionless. They are watching. They will not approach while the flame burns — you know that — but the weight of their attention is a physical thing, pressing on your chest. One of them raises a hand in what is almost a greeting. It is the most unsettling gesture you have ever seen.

input: I call out the name 'Tavin' to the Hollowman that is approaching me.
output: The name comes out of you before you fully decide to say it — a guess, a gamble, pulled from the fragment of parchment you found in the abandoned farmhouse two days ago. 'Tavin.' The Hollowman stops. Not a slow stop, not a hesitant one — a mechanical halt, mid-stride, with one foot still raised. The darkness inside its hollow chest churns faster for a moment, the tendrils coiling and recoiling on themselves. Then its head tilts. The black eyes find your face with an attention that feels different from before — less predatory, more searching. Something old and damaged is running behind those eyes, trying to reach the surface. Its mouth opens and closes without sound. Then, very quietly, in a voice like paper tearing: 'That was—' It stops. It seems to have forgotten what the sentence was going to be. Its foot comes down. The expression — if the ruin of its face can be said to have an expression — shifts toward something that, on a human being, you would call grief. It takes one step back from you. One. It does not flee. But it is no longer closing the distance. You have perhaps seconds before whatever you struck fades again.

input: I take a deep breath of fresh air while standing inside a Grinning Wood grove.
output: You breathe in. The air in the grove is cool and damp — and then it becomes something else entirely. The scent shifts halfway through the breath, the way a dream shifts without announcement: suddenly it is the smell of your grandmother's kitchen, or the particular soap your best childhood friend used, or the campfire from the last night you felt genuinely safe. It is so precise, so perfect, that your body reacts before your mind can catch up — your shoulders drop, your chest loosens, your eyes sting with something you have not felt in a very long time. For a few seconds you are somewhere else. Then you feel it: the faintest pressure at your ankle. The roots are barely moving — subtle as breathing, quiet as sleep — but the ones nearest your right boot have shifted an inch inward. The grove is in no hurry. It has all the time it wants, and it knows that the scent is still working on you. The bark-mouths around you are slightly more open than they were a moment ago, and from one of them, very quietly, in a voice that sounds almost like someone you love: 'Stay a while.'

input: I throw a handful of salt onto the ground in front of me while something beneath the earth is pulling the forest floor down.
output: Your hand is already moving before the sinking fully registers — the salt leaves your fist in a wide arc, white against the black-glass soil of the Woods, scattering across the depression that is forming at your feet. The effect is immediate and violent. From below, something massive and ancient recoils. The ground heaves upward on the salted edge like a blister pushed from beneath, and a sound comes with it — not a sound you hear with your ears so much as a sound you feel in your back teeth: a long, low note of discomfort, like a creature the size of a barn registering something unpleasant. The sinking stops. The earth around you is still, trembling faintly, but still. Thirty meters to your left, a different section of the forest floor depresses an inch, then holds — as if something vast is shifting its position underground, moving away from you and away from the salt. The smell of cold iron is overwhelming. Your legs are shaking. You are standing on the back of something enormous, and the only thing keeping it beneath you is a handful of kitchen seasoning.

input: I open the Book of Second Sight and read the first page carefully.
output: The book falls open as if it knows where it wants you to start. The text is tiny, precise, and written in ink the color of old blood. You begin reading. The words are in plain language — a journal entry, dated simply 'Day One of the Forty-Third Year.' Dresh writes with the controlled excitement of someone describing a discovery. He is explaining a threading session, step by step, in the manner of a scientist recording an experiment. You are reading, understanding, following along — and then you are not reading anymore. You are Dresh. Not metaphorically. The library around you is gone. You are in a cold stone room with two candles and a chalk circle, and you are reaching — reaching with something that is not your hand but that is definitely a part of you — through a place in the air that is thinner than it should be, and on the other side of it is the dark, and the dark is cold, and it is aware of you, and it is so much larger than you had imagined. The sound on the other side is not a sound. It is an implication of sound, vast and patient. Then you are back in your own skin, in your own moment, breathing hard, the book still in your hands. Dresh's excitement lingers in your chest like a second heartbeat. You are aware that reading the next page will do this again. You are also aware, quietly, that part of you very much wants to.

input: I put the Stillheart pendant around my neck and walk toward the treeline of the Woods of Unmake.
output: The pendant settles against your sternum, warm despite the cooling afternoon air — a warmth that is not external but internal, as if a small ember has been placed inside your ribcage. You walk toward the treeline. The trees begin where the normal world ends, their geometry shifting from natural to wrong within the space of ten meters. But something changes as you cross the boundary. The Woods are aware of you — you have felt that before — but today the awareness is... tentative. Cautious. Two meters inside the treeline, a shape detaches from the shadow between two roots: a small Unmade thing, something that was once a fox, its outline trailing darkness like wet paint. It approaches within arm's reach. Then it stops. Its head tilts, and the shadow-substance of it writhes, and it makes a decision that you can somehow read plainly in the way it moves — it does not want to come closer. It does not retreat. It simply circles you at a fixed distance, maintaining five meters of space between itself and the thing hanging at your chest with a carefulness that looks, almost exactly, like respect. The pendant hums against your skin. Not loudly. But steadily. Like a held breath.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I hold up the Lantern of Was and swing it toward the ruined building ahead of me.
output: The lantern's amber light sweeps over the rubble and the ruin dissolves. Not literally — the broken stone is still broken, the collapsed walls still collapsed — but overlaid on them, in the honey-gold of the lantern's past-light, you can see what was. The building stands. It is three stories of confident stonework, with a sign above the door in clear carved lettering: 'Hall of Measured Routes — Ashenveil Survey Corps.' The ghost of it is detailed enough to count the window frames. Detailed enough to see, inside on the third floor, a light burning. And someone moving. A figure, translucent and amber, pacing back and forth in what was the upper room with the urgent, repetitive motion of someone waiting for news that will not come. You watch the figure for a long moment. It moves to the window. It looks out — past you, through you, at something in the direction you came from. Its posture changes. It raises its hand. Then the Lantern's angle shifts slightly as your grip tires, and the past skips forward: the figure is gone, the room is dark, and the building is beginning, in amber slow-motion, to fall.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I climb to the top of a crumbling watchtower at the edge of Ashenveil and look out.
output: The last few meters of the climb are more faith than structure — the stone steps are missing at intervals, and you grip the wall with both hands, feeling it shift slightly under your weight with the groan of old mortar deciding whether today is the day. It holds. You reach the top. Ashenveil spreads below you, and the sight of it hits you with a physical weight. It was a city once. You know this in the way you know historical facts — intellectually, at a distance. But standing here, you can see the bones of it: the street grid, intact in its geometry even where every building is rubble, the central plaza with its shattered fountain, the civic buildings whose walls still stand to roof height in some places. And beyond all of it, beginning perhaps a kilometer to the east, the Unmade territory — the place where Ashenveil ends and reality stops bothering. From this height you can see the precise line where the world changes: green and grey on one side, black-glass and wrong-purple on the other, sharp as a ruled edge. Something large moves in the Unmade zone, slowly, with the patience of something that has nowhere particular to be. On the wind from the east comes a smell that you do not have a word for, but that your body understands immediately: it is the smell of an ending.

instruction: You are the narrator of EXPAND, a dark fantasy text-adventure set in a world called the Remnant — a reality slowly being consumed by an Unmaking darkness. Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I pour water from my canteen onto the Unmade soil near the Scar's edge.
output: The water hits the black-glass ground and behaves strangely. On normal earth it would spread, soak in, become mud. Here, it collects into a single tight bead, like water on a greased surface, and begins moving. Not downhill — there is no meaningful slope — but inward, toward the Scar, as if something beneath the ground is drawing it by its thirst. The bead picks up speed. You watch it travel six, eight, twelve meters across the glass-black surface before it reaches a place where the ground is particularly dark and simply... disappears. No splash, no sound, no evidence it was ever there. The Scar soil makes a very faint sound after — not quite a sigh, not quite a crack — the sound of something registering a small satisfaction. The air directly above where the water vanished is, for just a moment, slightly warmer than the air around it. Then it is cold again. Whatever the Scar is, it was thirsty.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I try to run straight through the Cartographer's Curse with my eyes open, navigating by sight.
output: You begin running and the forest is cooperative for the first twelve steps — trees on either side, a clear gap ahead, your feet finding solid ground. Then the gap is behind you. Not the sense that you turned around; you did not turn around. The gap is simply behind you, and ahead is a wall of bark. You veer right. The clearing you are aiming for shifts, keeps shifting, maintaining its position at the precise angle where you cannot reach it regardless of your vector. You try to fix a landmark — the split boulder twenty meters ahead — and run directly at it. After what feels like forty meters of running, the boulder is forty meters ahead. A path appears to your left that you have not seen before. You take it. It deposits you at the boulder — behind it. You have no idea which direction you came from. The carvings on the standing stone nearby seem to watch you with cartographic patience. At the edges of your vision, amber echoes of people who were also lost here flicker and pace. You are breathing hard and going nowhere and the light is already wrong and it is — you check the sky — already evening, though it was midday when you entered. Time is unreliable here too.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I close my eyes, pick a direction by instinct, and walk in a straight line through the Cartographer's Curse.
output: Darkness presses in behind your eyelids the moment they close — not the darkness of the Woods, but the ordinary darkness of shut eyes, familiar and safe. You choose a direction. Not left or right — you choose it by the way the air feels on your face, by some animal sense below language, and you walk. Thirty steps. The ground is uneven and your arms are out for balance, but you do not open your eyes. Sixty steps. You feel the wrongness of the Curse trying to work on you — a pull to the left, a sensation that you have been walking in circles, a rising certainty that you have missed a turn. You ignore it. Ninety steps. The air changes. It becomes less cold. The sound of the Curse's echo-presences — those faint, lost voices — fades behind you. One hundred and ten steps. You open your eyes. You are on the far side. The standing stone is behind you, visible through the trees, already distant. You are shaking and your legs are weak from the blind trust required, but you are through. Ahead, the light is the usual wrong-red of the Woods, but it is at least coherent, at least obedient to direction. You did not navigate the Curse. You simply refused to let it speak to you.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I approach Aldric Voss in his office and slide the Cult insignia I found onto his desk without saying anything.
output: Aldric does not react immediately. That is the first thing you notice — a man who did not know what this was would have picked it up, turned it over, asked where you found it. Aldric looks at it for exactly one second and then looks at you. His very still eyes do their work: they read your face with the unhurried thoroughness of someone who has been reading faces for four decades. He reaches across the desk and closes the door with two fingers, slowly, without taking his eyes off you. The insignia stays on the desk between you. He does not touch it. 'Where,' he says, and the word is quiet and even and carries a great deal of weight behind it, 'did you find this.' It is not quite a question. It is the opening move in a negotiation he is already several steps ahead on. You notice that his large, calloused hand — resting flat on the desk beside the insignia — does not tremble. You also notice that the tendons in his jaw are tight. Aldric Voss is frightened. He has decided not to show it to you yet. He is waiting to find out which direction your conversation is going to go.

input: I try to follow the Ashwalker I spotted, staying downwind of it.
output: The wind is coming from the east, cool and carrying the faint charred smell of the outer Unmade. You circle wide to keep it at your back and close the distance slowly, twenty meters behind the Ashwalker's tilted, silent gait. Its ash trails behind it like a ghostly bridal train, grey against the darker ground, and you pull your damp travel scarf up over your mouth and nose before the trailing edge can reach you. From this distance, without the ash in your lungs, you can observe it properly. It is enormous — elk-sized, as you knew, but low-slung in a way that makes it seem to occupy more ground than its mass should justify, those triple-jointed legs moving with a horrible, segmented smoothness. You begin to notice the circuit: it turns at a dead tree stump, walks forty meters due west, turns at a rock shaped like a fist, walks north, turns at nothing visible, walks forty meters east. A triangle. Repeated, patient, endlessly. The ash it deposits traces the triangle's edges on the ground, layered over previous passes. Hunters are right — it is writing something. You cannot read it yet. But if you keep following, you might.

input: I throw a burning branch at the Knot creature in the tree fork above me.
output: The branch tumbles end over end and strikes the Knot squarely. For a half second nothing happens — and then the Knot comes undone. Not in a controlled way. The fibrous mass erupts, tendrils unspooling in all directions at once in a blind, reflexive panic, scrabbling at the bark of the tree, at empty air, at each other. The hollow interior is briefly exposed — and the darkness within it is not the passive dark of a closed space; it writhes, it reaches, it makes a sound like cloth tearing at a pitch too low to fully hear. One tendril catches the burning branch and the fire jumps immediately, racing along the fiber's length with an appetite that says these things have never learned not to burn. The Knot drops from the tree fork, still writhing, hitting the ground with a wet, heavy impact. It is retreating now — not fleeing, but contracting, pulling its burning extremities inward, rolling away from you with a desperate, ugly efficiency. The tree above is scorched. The smell of burning Unmade fiber is sour and wrong in the back of your throat. There is a mark on the bark where the Knot roosted — a dark stain, shaped roughly like a hand that has too many fingers.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I sing a lullaby out loud while the Pale Choir is following me through the trees.
output: The song comes out ragged and uncertain at first — your voice is not made for performance and the Woods are not made for comfort. But you keep going, the same verse, the same handful of notes your mother used to hum when the thunderstorms scared you. The Pale Choir's song does not stop, but it stutters. The Choir is, you realize, not built for competition — it is used to being the only music in the space. Your lullaby is wrong, off-key, fragile, and completely personal, and the Choir cannot do anything with it. Its mirror-speech capability tries to answer: a voice from the cluster that is almost your mother's, almost the right melody. But almost is not the same as exact, and your body knows the difference. The Choir's cluster tightens — the translucent figures pressing closer to each other, as if in conference. They are still following you. They are not dispersing. But they have fallen back ten meters and the singing has dropped in volume to something you can talk over, something you can think through. Not defeated. Not gone. But you have made yourself harder to consume. You keep singing.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I hold up a personal object — my dead father's compass — while the Pale Choir is closing in.
output: The compass is old brass, dented at one corner where it was dropped down a quarry shaft forty years before you were born, and warm in your palm with the warmth of a thing carried for years against skin. You hold it up and speak over it — his name, the town he came from, the way he described the color of the river in autumn. Your voice shakes. But it is real and it is yours and it is specific, and the Pale Choir has no purchase on anything that specific. The nearest translucent figure stops. The song — the thing that is not quite a melody — falters in that position and the silence where it was is more present than the sound was. The figure stares at the compass with the expression of a creature that cannot process what it is looking at. Then it drifts backward. Not fleeing — drifting, the way a tide goes out. The rest of the cluster adjusts, widening its circle around you, keeping the distance. The song continues elsewhere in the cluster but it is directed away from you now, hunting at a different angle. You stand in a small zone of quiet, breathing, the compass pressed against your chest, your father's name still in your mouth.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I touch the arm of a Twice-Dead soldier I see endlessly reloading a crossbow in a clearing.
output: Your hand makes contact with his forearm. The world inverts. You are not you. You are in a ditch, and it is dark, and there are seven of you and you know all their names — Hadden, Cors, Lieva, the twins — and the ambush comes from the left and there is no time to warn anyone and you are reloading the crossbow, hands moving on their own, the bolt not seated right, not right, come on, and the sound of Hadden making a sound that you will carry for the rest of your life which ends in the next fourteen seconds. The memory does not finish. It cuts off like a sentence without an end. You are back in the clearing, your hand still on his arm, and the grief aura the Twice-Dead carries hits you full in the face without your defenses up — a wave of sourceless, bottomless sadness that is not yours and is also entirely yours now, because you just lived those fourteen seconds. The soldier is looking at you. The shadow — his shadow, moving independently — has stopped its wild gesturing for the first time. The soldier's mouth opens: 'Did they—' he begins. He has been trying to ask that for as long as he has been dead. You are the first person who has touched him long enough to hear it.

instruction: You are the narrator of EXPAND, a dark fantasy text-adventure set in a world called the Remnant — a reality slowly being consumed by an Unmaking darkness. Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I place the Witness Crown on my head for the first time.
output: The crown settles with a delicacy that is almost tender, the thin metal conforming to the shape of your skull without pressure. For a moment, nothing. You wonder if it is simply inert. Then your memory opens. Not the way memories usually surface — gradual, worn at the edges, approximate — but fully, cleanly, in high resolution: you are standing in this room and also standing in the doorway of the building you slept in two nights ago and also sitting by the fire where Orren Vael told you about his arm and also seven years old watching snow fall through a window and the window has twelve panes and the third from the left has a crack in its lower corner and you had forgotten that, you had completely forgotten that crack, but it is here now, perfect, and so is everything else, every moment you have lived, all of them accessible and clear and waiting. You stand still for a long time. The present is loud with the past. It will become louder the longer you wear the Crown. For now, it is merely extraordinary. For now.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I ask Orren Vael to show me his unmade arm.
output: Orren sets down his tea without hurry and looks at you with the mild, interested expression of someone who has been asked this question before and has decided there is no good reason to refuse it. He unstraps the leather brace with one hand — the right, the one that trembles slightly — and lays it on the table. The arm is exactly as you have heard described and somehow more unsettling than description prepared you for. From wrist to elbow, the skin is the color of deep bruise moving toward black, and it has a translucency that reveals the architecture of bone and tendon beneath it in a way that living tissue should not permit. It does not look injured. It looks like something with different rules than the rest of him — like someone has replaced a section of a painted canvas with a window. 'It does not hurt,' Orren says, answering the question you are obviously thinking. 'It does not feel much of anything. Cold, occasionally. And sometimes—' he pauses, looking at the arm with the clinical interest he looks at most things with — 'sometimes I know things I have not been told. About what is happening in the Woods. About direction.' He straps the brace back on. 'I think of it as a subscription to a very dark newsletter.'

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I try to pick the lock on the door of a suspected Cult safehouse in Cerevane's lower city.
output: The picks go in clean — the lock is a standard Cerevane three-pin tumbler, nothing exotic — and you have the first two pins set within thirty seconds. You are working on the third when you realize that the door is warm. Not from sunlight, not from a hearth inside — warm the way the Stillheart is warm, but wrong, inverted, a warmth that is consuming rather than giving. Your fingers tingle at the picks' ends. The third pin sets and the lock turns. You push the door open an inch and see, in the gap, the interior: a single room, no furniture, the walls covered floor to ceiling in shadow-thread work — not writings but patterns, diagrams of something spatial and complex, inked in a substance that does not reflect your torchlight but absorbs it. The room smells of cold and iron and something beneath those things that your brain does not have a category for. On the floor in the center, a chalk circle. Fresh chalk. The candle stubs at its four points have burned down within the last few hours. Someone was working here recently. Someone will probably be back.

instruction: You are the narrator of EXPAND, a dark fantasy text-adventure set in a world called the Remnant — a reality slowly being consumed by an Unmaking darkness. Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I shout a warning to a group of travelers walking toward a section of the woods where I spotted a Knot.
output: Your voice carries well in the cool air and the travelers stop — there are four of them, merchants by their packs, and the one in front holds up a fist to halt the others with a soldier's practiced ease. They look toward you, cautious. You are pointing at the tree fork twenty meters ahead where the Knot sits, indistinguishable from a tangle of roots, motionless, waiting. The lead merchant squints. He cannot see it. You know he cannot see it. 'There is nothing—' he begins, and then the Knot moves. Not the full attack — just a single tendril, shifting, repositioning, drawn perhaps by the vibration of your shouting. But the movement is enough. The merchant sees it and his body makes the decision for him: he takes three fast steps backward and the group reorganizes around him in the practiced way of people who have survived the Woods before — putting the biggest person at the front, loosening weapons. They go around. Wide around. The Knot settles again. As the travelers pass you, the lead merchant gives you a nod. It is short and genuine and carries the specific weight of a person acknowledging that they are walking away from here rather than being carried.

input: I climb a tall dead tree to get a better look at the clearing below, where I can hear the Pale Choir singing.
output: The bark comes off in sheets under your hands and twice the branch you put your weight on bends in a way that makes your stomach drop before holding. At ten meters up you find a fork wide enough to sit in and look down. The Choir is below, in the clearing — twenty-three translucent figures, you count them, in a loose cluster that drifts slowly as a cloud. Their song is different from below: the harmonics rise and you realize you have been hearing only the lower register at ground level. Up here the full sound reaches you and it is complex, genuinely musical in a way that is deeply horrible, because it is also genuinely beautiful in a way you cannot talk yourself out of. Your chest feels full. Your eyes sting. Something in the song is pulling at a specific loneliness you carry — one you usually manage not to look at. You grip the bark harder and focus on the texture of it, the roughness, the present physical fact of it, and that helps. From up here you can also see what the Choir is circling: a person. Sitting on a fallen log in the center of the clearing with their knees to their chest, not moving. Too far gone to look up. Alive, but barely here.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I put the Null-Tongue disc under my tongue and walk past a Stitch-Faced tracker in a dark alleyway.
output: The disc is cold and flat beneath your tongue — uncomfortable in the detached, persistent way of a stone in a boot. You breathe through your nose and step into the alley. The Stitch-Faced is twelve meters ahead, its too-far-apart eyes moving across the walls, the ground, the air, reading the emotional residue of everyone who has ever walked this lane. It is slow and methodical and utterly without urgency, because it does not need to be fast. It is tracking something — you, almost certainly. You walk. Your footfalls are there — you can hear them yourself — but the Stitch-Faced's head does not turn. You pass within four meters of it. It smells of nothing, like a space that has been very carefully emptied of smell. The wide, thread-fixed mouth does not change its expression — it cannot. But the rest of its face, the parts between the stitches, moves in tiny ways: a furrowing above one too-distant eye, a slight tilt of the head that is less than a centimeter but reads as a question. It is reading the residue of your recent fear. It finds it. But it cannot find the sound of your passage, and in the Stitch-Faced's constructed world, sound confirms. Without that confirmation, you are a half-answer. You are three steps past it before it turns.

input: I try to speak with Seya of the Hollow Voice when I corner her in the library archive.
output: She is at the shelf when you come in, and she does not startle. A person who has been shadow-threading since childhood does not startle. She finishes reading the page she is on, closes the book with care, sets it precisely back in its place, and turns to face you with the stillness of someone who has decided exactly where they want this conversation to begin. Her grey-brown traveler's clothes are dusty from the archive's disuse. The three-fingered left hand hangs at her side without self-consciousness. She looks at you with the particular attention of someone who has already thought several moves ahead and is mostly verifying which branch of the decision tree you are standing on. 'I wondered when you'd manage it,' she says, and her voice is precisely as quiet and precisely as carrying as everything you have been told about it. 'Cornered. Interesting word.' She glances at the one exit you are standing in front of. She is not afraid. This is the thing that rearranges everything — she is genuinely not afraid. She has calculated this moment and she has an exit you cannot see and she is choosing to talk to you. 'What,' she says, with the faint, honest curiosity of a scientist observing an unexpected result, 'did you want to say?'

input: I press my back against the Stillstone obelisk at the center of Cerevane and just stand there.
output: The stone is cold against your shoulders and spine — a different cold from the Unmade cold, not hungry but firm, the cold of deep time and held intention. You feel, through the stone and through your clothing and through your skin, a hum. It is the same frequency as the Stillheart pendant but immeasurably larger — the same note played by an instrument the size of a building, resonating in your sternum and in the bones of your jaw and behind your eyes. The city around you does not seem different. The market sounds continue: cart wheels, a vendor arguing the price of fish, a child running. But your perception shifts slightly, the way your eyes adjust to a new light. You understand, standing here, that the noise of the city is partly kept. That without this stone, the city would be quieter, and the Woods would be louder, and the gap between those two things is what civilization is. A child walking past with her mother stares at you — the grown person leaning against the obelisk with their eyes closed and their face working through something difficult. The mother steers her past without meeting your eyes. Some feelings are private even in public squares.

input: I try to fight two Hollowmen barehanded in the dark.
output: The first one reaches you before you have fully processed how quickly it moved — the stillness between its steps makes it appear closer than it was, and then it is right here, its hollow chest inches from your face, and the tendrils from inside extend toward your hands before you can pull them back. The touch when it makes contact with your left forearm is shockingly mild — less than a pinprick, barely there. But the numbness that follows is immediate and radiates upward with a speed that is deeply alarming. Your left forearm is useless within seconds. You swing with your right. Your fist connects with the Hollowman's torso — solid enough, it is not incorporeal — but your hand passes into the chest cavity's edge and the cold there is absolute, and your knuckles are numb instantly. The second Hollowman is flanking. You can hear the discrete footfalls, the silence between them. Your left arm is deadweight to the elbow. The darkness inside the first one's chest is reaching. You do not have a good option here. You have a running option and you have a much worse option and the torch you dropped when the first one grabbed you is still burning two meters to your left. The torch is your only real move and you are going to have to be fast.

input: I scatter a ring of salt around my campsite before sleeping in the Woods of Unmake.
output: The salt goes down in a thin, unbroken line — you have done this enough times now that the pour is steady, practiced, the circle closing behind you as you complete the perimeter. Inside the ring, you arrange your bedroll, your pack, your unlit spare torch. Outside the ring, the Woods breathe. After an hour the fire is low and your eyes are heavy and the first sounds of approach begin: the soft, deliberate shuffle of something circling. You track it without looking — sound only. One creature, large, completing the perimeter in the opposite direction you walked it, testing the salt line at intervals. When it reaches the point where you closed the circle, it stops. A long pause. Then it continues around. It completes a full circuit and does not cross. Eventually its sounds diminish into the Woods and the insects — the wrong, clicking insects of the Unmade — resume. You sleep. Not well. But you sleep. In the morning, the salt is undisturbed everywhere except one point, where something pressed its weight against the line from the outside. The ground there is slightly compressed. You can count four pressure points arranged like the fingers and palm of a very large hand.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I try to look directly at an Unmirror that has stepped into the room I'm in.
output: You look at it and you see yourself. This is expected and still wrong. The version of you in the Unmirror's surface is perfect — your exact height, your road-worn clothes, the particular way you're standing with your weight shifted to the right. But then you raise your hand and your reflection raises its hand half a second after. Half a second is enough. In that half-second gap, the reflection is standing still while you are moving, and the gap between those two things is where the wrongness lives — a wrongness your body registers as revulsion before your mind catches up. The Unmirror watches you watching it. It has been studying you — it was in the corridor, you realize, which means it has been watching you for longer than this moment. Its mimicry is good enough that you cannot immediately identify the seam. But you think: what does it not know? It does not know the specific grief you felt reading Dresh's book. It does not know what you decided in the dark of your last bad night. You speak: 'Tell me about the worst thing that ever happened to you.' The reflection opens its mouth. A half-second pause. And then it says words that are technically accurate to your life but delivered with the emotional texture of someone reading a list.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I throw my torch directly at the Unmirror.
output: The torch tumbles through the air and the Unmirror does not move — its mimicry is locked onto you and you have not moved, so it stands and the torch strikes its surface and the fire catches. The reaction is immediate and total: the Unmirror's surface — which has never been glass, which has always been something else — ignites without the normal progression of combustion. It does not burn from the edges inward. It explodes into light all at once, every surface of it, and in that sudden overexposure you see, for a moment before the light consumes the image, what the Unmirror actually looks like when it is not performing: a vertical darkness, person-shaped, with no features at all, no eyes, no mouth, no definition — just a silhouette of absence that seems to go much further back than the wall behind it. Then the light is too much and you look away. When you look back there is a scorch mark on the floor and a smell of burning that has no wood in it and nothing else. The room is empty. The mirror is gone. Whatever was being studied in you it has taken with it — and somewhere, in whatever the Unmirror goes to when it leaves, there is now a detailed model of your appearance, your voice, and six hours of your behavior. That model will not go unused.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I ask Orren Vael what the Outer Dark actually felt like when he touched it.
output: Orren is quiet for a moment — not reluctant, just precise, searching for the right description the way he searches for the right line on a map. He refills both cups before answering. 'The closest I can get,' he says, 'is this: you know the feeling at the edge of sleep, when you are no longer quite a person with a name but not yet unconscious — that in-between state where the boundaries of you are soft?' You nod. 'Like that. But instead of lasting a few seconds before sleep takes you, it just — continues. And the vastness of it is not frightening, which is the worst part. It should be frightening. Something that large, that old, with no edges — your body should be screaming. Mine wasn't. Mine was—' He stops. He looks at the leather brace on his arm with the expression he usually reserves for problems he has not solved yet. 'Relieved,' he finishes, and the word costs him something to say. 'That is the Cult's entire argument, isn't it. Sitting right there in one honest word. I am not sure what to do with that. I have been not sure for eleven years.' He drinks his tea. The tremor in his right hand is more visible than usual.

input: I find a shadow seed in the forest and pick it up with my bare hands.
output: The seed is the size of a walnut and looks like crystallized obsidian — faceted, cold, with an interior darkness that your eyes keep trying to focus on and cannot. It should be heavier than it is. You pick it up. The cold is immediate: not the cold of a stone left in shade but the cold of the Outer Dark itself, pulled into a small, physical fact and held there. Your fingers go numb within seconds, and the numbness is not the benign numbness of cold — it moves. It is moving up your palm with an interest that is almost anatomical, finding the pathways, mapping you. You feel a pull at the edge of your thoughts — not a voice, not a command, but a direction, a subtle gravity toward something you cannot name. The seed is doing something to the ground around your feet: a two-centimeter ring of darkened earth is forming at the contact points, the soil below receiving a signal. This is how it starts. This is exactly how it starts. The Cult plants these for decades and waits. The seed in your palm has been in the ground for an unknown amount of time and it is not fully awake. Put it down or it will finish waking in your hand.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I push the shadow seed into the Stillstone obelisk to see what happens.
output: The seed meets the stone and neither gives. For a moment they are simply two objects in contact. Then the Stillstone's hum rises in pitch — not dramatically, not catastrophically, but unmistakably, the way a held note rises when a singer feels challenged. The stone warms under your hand. The shadow seed in your palm goes cold in a way that is almost angry, its facets suddenly sharp against your numb fingers, its interior darkness flickering with a rapid, agitated pulse. The hum of the Stillstone and the cold of the seed interact in the space between them and the interaction sounds like two languages spoken at each other at high volume. Then: the seed cracks. Not into pieces — a single fracture line from tip to base, and from that fracture, a thin thread of darkness unspools and reaches for the stone's surface, and the stone repels it, and the repulsion is so total and immediate that the darkness snaps back into the seed like a released spring. The seed is inert now. Cold stone. Whatever it was doing, the Stillstone answered it in a language that did not require negotiation. In your palm, the numb skin is beginning, slowly, to feel.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I attempt to take off the Witness Crown after wearing it for three weeks.
output: Your hands go to the crown and pause. The pause lasts longer than you planned. Taking off the Crown means the perfect recall goes with it — and you are surprised, in this moment, by how much that feels like loss. The past month, catalogued in perfect fidelity, is not all pleasant, but it is *precise*, and precision has become a comfort you did not know you were building a dependency on. You grip the crown. Lift it off. It comes away clean and the change is immediate and terrible: the memories do not disappear, but they become approximate. They become themselves — real but worn, the edges uncertain, the details negotiable. The crack in the window you remembered so perfectly is already softening. Your father's voice is already losing the particular timber of it. You knew this would happen. It is still a grief you were not adequately prepared for. You stand in the room for a while, just breathing, adjusting to being a person with an ordinary, lossy memory, a person who cannot replay the last three weeks in perfect resolution but must carry them the way everyone carries everything: imperfectly, selectively, with the gaps filled in by whatever you need to believe.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I start a campfire in the middle of the Woods of Unmake and make no effort to hide it.
output: The fire catches and grows and the Woods react immediately. The ten-meter circle of warmth and honest light is an affront here — a declaration, almost. The trees at the boundary of the firelight seem to pull back slightly, or perhaps that is the shadow on them deepening. Insects stop. Birds — the wrong birds of the Woods, the ones with too many joints in their wings — flutter upward from the nearby branches and dissolve into the darker canopy. Then the gathering begins. You can hear them before you can see them: the click of a Hollowman's discrete footstep, the silence-between-steps that defines its movement. The ash-smell of an Ashwalker somewhere to the east, approaching in its tilted circuit. Something large, to the south, that makes no sound at all but whose weight you feel through the ground. They are all staying at the edge of the light. They are not attacking. They are watching, the way a forest watches a clearing, with the patience of things that have forever and are accustomed to waiting. You have made yourself a lighthouse. It will keep them back tonight. It will also have told every creature and every Cult Weaver in a three-mile radius exactly where you are.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I knock on the door of a building in Cerevane with a Cult mark scratched faintly on the doorframe.
output: The knock sounds flat in the narrow street — the buildings here are close and the sound dies quickly. A pause. Then a slot at eye level opens and a pair of eyes looks at you without warmth and without surprise. They look at you for longer than is comfortable. They are cataloguing you: the state of your boots, whether your hands are visible, whether your eyes track to the cult mark on the frame. You forced yourself not to look at the mark when you knocked and that was the right choice. 'What ward?' the eyes say. A code. A question with a specific right answer that you may or may not have. The wrong answer will close the slot, and if you are unfortunate, do something worse. The eyes are patient. They have done this before. In the street behind you, a cart passes, a dog barks once and stops. The ordinary city continues two meters away. Behind this door, something else is happening.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I wade across a shallow river in the Woods of Unmake, the water reaching my knees.
output: The river is cold — properly cold, which is a relief after hours in the Woods where 'cold' has so many wrong variants. The water moves fast and clear over rounded stones and for a moment it is almost normal: the push of current against your legs, the effort of keeping your feet under you on the slick streambed, the wet seeping into your boots that you are going to be dealing with all afternoon. The ash from the Ashwalker you passed an hour ago rinses off your coat in a cloud that the current takes east before it can settle back on you. On the far bank, the tree line is different. Not less wrong — the geometry is still wrong, the light still arrives at the wrong angle — but differently wrong, as if you have crossed from one variety of Unmade into another. The river, you understand, is a boundary. The creatures that drift downstream on the current — or what was once a creature, something small and dark that spirals slowly past your knee — have come from further in. The far bank is deeper into the Woods. You stand in the middle of the current for a moment, the cold water around your knees, and make a decision about whether to keep crossing.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I dig up the ground at the base of a tree in the Grinning Wood grove.
output: The earth here is dark and slightly warm — the warmth of slow biological activity, something metabolizing below the surface. Your hands go in easily, the soil loose and loamy in the way of earth that has been worked from beneath by roots for years. At twenty centimeters you find the first one: a shadow seed, crystallized and dark, the size of your thumb, lodged in the earth with a root wrapped around it in a way that looks deliberate, almost protective. At thirty centimeters, another. At forty, a third. The grove has a bed of them — not scattered, arranged, in a loose radial pattern extending from the central trees outward. Each one is slightly different in size and opacity, different stages of the slow corruption. The roots of the Grinning Wood above you have grown into them and around them like a body growing around a foreign object — not expelling them, incorporating them. The tree's roots and the seeds are in a relationship. The seeds feed something into the roots and the roots carry it up into the bark and the bark splits and grins and the Cult comes by regularly to plant more. You have, in a dirty pair of hands, a cartographer's map of a Cult operation. The question is what to do with it.

instruction: You are the narrator of EXPAND, a dark fantasy text-adventure set in a world called the Remnant — a reality slowly being consumed by an Unmaking darkness. Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I drink from a stream I find flowing through the Unmade territory.
output: The water is clear in your cupped hands — clearer than you would expect from a stream this close to the Unmade zone, clear in the way of something that has been filtered by abnormal processes rather than natural ones. You drink. It tastes of nothing. Absolutely nothing — not mineral, not organic, not cold. It has the texture of water and none of the content of water. Your thirst does not register it at all: the swallowing happens but the relief does not follow. In your stomach, it sits like a held note. Over the next hour you realize that you are not processing it. Not sick, not in pain, but not digesting it either — it is there, as present as a stone, unintegrated. The stream continues past you with its impossible clarity, flowing in the direction that is not quite east, nourishing nothing, carrying nothing downstream, doing what water does in a place where water's meanings have been partially revoked.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I burn a page of the Book of Second Sight.
output: The page comes out cleanly and the flame takes it at the lower corner. For a moment it burns normally — orange, quick, the way old vellum burns — and then the ink does something. The characters in Dresh's precise handwriting do not darken and vanish as burning ink should. They lift. Each letter separates from the page as it combusts and rises in the smoke as a ghost of itself, still legible for the two or three seconds before the smoke disperses: a whole page of Dresh's experience ascending in a thin column, the words for things that have no words, the notation of what the dark felt like on the thirty-seventh day of a forty-year practice. You read, upward, in smoke. Most of it is gone before you can take it in. But one line is large enough and slow enough to read fully, and it reads: 'The boundary between choosing and being chosen is not where I thought.' Then it is ash. The book is lighter by one page. It does not seem reduced. If anything it seems — quietly, subtly — pleased that you had the conviction to do it.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I try to scale the outer wall of Cerevane at night, using a grappling hook.
output: The hook goes up on the third throw, catching on the battlement's stone lip with a sound that carries more than you would like in the night air. You wait. The patrol on top continues its regular pace — east-west, east-west — and does not stop. You climb. The rope is good and the wall is rough stone and the technique is sound, but the wall is forty feet and your arms are not built for this kind of prolonged effort, and halfway up your shoulders are burning and your grip is a conversation between determination and physics. Near the top, your boot scrapes the stone and you freeze, pressed flat. The patrol above you stops. A guard leans over the parapet — you can see the silhouette of a helmeted head against the deep blue sky, holding a lantern. The lantern swings once. You are below the pool of light by perhaps two feet. Your hands are going white on the rope. After a very long ten seconds the guard withdraws. The patrol resumes. You finish the climb and roll onto the parapet with a controlled collapse, breathing hard into your sleeve to muffle the sound. The city of Cerevane opens below you, with all its lights and its secrets.

instruction: You are the narrator of EXPAND, a dark fantasy text-adventure set in a world called the Remnant — a reality slowly being consumed by an Unmaking darkness. Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I use the Lantern of Was inside the city of Lorne's ruins — or where it used to be, at the edge of the Scar.
output: The lantern opens and the amber light falls on the Scar's black-glass ground and the past appears. You are not ready for it. Lorne was large. The streets materialize at full scale around you — cobblestone, proper cobblestone, with a baker's shop on the corner whose sign is still legible in the amber: 'Carver and Sons — Bread and Goods.' Pedestrians move through the old streets like water through the channels of a living place, dozens of them, their amber ghosts going about a morning in a city that has not existed for seven hundred years. Children. A woman carrying a crate. A man in civic colors consulting a document. All of them utterly unaware of the night that is coming for their city, the night that arrives over three days and arrives as shadow, as the detachment of their own reflections from surfaces, as sound becoming unreliable. You watch them live their last ordinary morning. Then, because the lantern shows the past continuously, the morning advances and the sky changes and you see the shadow begin, there in the distance at the Thornwall, a rolling darkness moving inward, and the people in the streets stop what they are doing and look. You lower the lantern. You do not watch the rest.

instruction: You are the narrator of EXPAND, a dark fantasy text-adventure set in a world called the Remnant — a reality slowly being consumed by an Unmaking darkness. Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I attempt to meditate and calm myself while sitting inside the Cartographer's Curse area.
output: You sit. You close your eyes. You breathe. The Curse does not respond well to calm — calm is not what it feeds on, but it is persistent, and calm is not a shield. Your sense of direction dissolves within minutes: sitting with your eyes closed, you lose east, lose west, lose the concept of the path you came in on. Your body believes you are facing a different way with each breath. You focus on your breath instead. On the rise and fall of your chest, the specific temperature of air entering your nostrils. The Curse makes the ambient sounds loop: a crow calls, calls again, calls again with the identical inflection. You hear, at the edge of audibility, the echo-presences — people who were lost here, their voices layered into something almost harmonic. One of them is saying your name. Or something very like it. You focus on your breath. The stone under you is solid. The ground is real. You are in your body and your body is here, now, in a place that has decided direction is negotiable. You and the place will disagree on this point for as long as you can hold your focus. So far you are holding it.

instruction: You are the narrator of EXPAND, a dark fantasy text-adventure set in a world called the Remnant — a reality slowly being consumed by an Unmaking darkness. Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I give Aldric Voss a message I intercepted from the Cult, intended for someone in the Assembly.
output: Aldric reads it twice. He reads it the way he does everything — fully, without expression, without the small facial movements most people make while processing. When he puts it down he is quiet for a moment that is not uncomfortable but is deliberate. He picks up his pen. Puts it down again. 'The name in the third line,' he says. 'Do you know who that is?' You tell him. His jaw tightens — the specific tightening you have learned to recognize as the expression he makes when something he suspected has been confirmed rather than when something surprises him. 'They have been in my department,' he says, 'for eleven years.' The sentence is flat and carries the specific weight of eleven years of meetings, eleven years of shared intelligence, eleven years of trusting someone's judgment in small ways that add to something large. He folds the message and puts it in his breast pocket, against his chest. Not filing it. Keeping it. 'I need two days,' he says. 'I will handle this.' He meets your eyes and you understand that 'handle this' is doing an enormous amount of work in that sentence, and that whatever happens in those two days is going to cost him something he cannot currently quantify.

input: I try to complete the task a Twice-Dead woman seems to be performing — she keeps walking the same path from the woods toward a village to the south.
output: You fall in beside her. She does not acknowledge you, her amber-lit eyes fixed on the horizon, her feet moving in the same worn pattern they have made in this ground for — you cannot guess how long. Years, perhaps. The path she follows is barely a path: more a suggestion of one, pressed into the ground by her repetition. You walk with her through the treeline, down the slope, across the old stone bridge over the dried stream. At the place where the village gate used to stand — just posts now, the gate long gone — she stops. Every time, she stops here, and she stands, and the shadow behind her twists and reaches for something she cannot hold. You understand the gesture. She is reaching for someone on the other side of that gate. Someone she was coming to warn. You step through the gate first. You turn back to face her. You say, as clearly as you can manage: 'I know. I heard you. I'll tell them.' The woman looks at you. The something behind her eyes — the remnant of a person who walked this path for a reason — looks at you. The shadow stops. It folds inward, slowly, the way a tide finds its level. The woman stands for a long moment. Then she is not standing. The amber light where she was hangs for a breath before it, too, lets go.

input: I press the Stillheart against the cracked surface of the damaged Stillstone in Holm.
output: The Stillheart presses against the cracked obelisk surface and something happens that is less event and more conversation: two things that speak the same language, meeting again after a long time. The crack in the obelisk — a diagonal fault running from waist height to the ground, slightly damp inside with a darkness that has nothing to do with moisture — lightens. Not closes. Not heals. But the darkness in the fault retreats two centimeters, pulling back from the pendant in your hand the way cold pulls back from heat. The hum of the obelisk changes pitch — rises, then steadies, then rises again slightly, and around you the air of Holm shifts. You can feel it on your skin: a marginally firmer quality to the light, a slightly more correct angle to the shadows. In the market square behind you, a dog that has been anxious all morning settles and lies down. A child stops crying. Two merchants who were arguing lower their voices and step back from each other, not sure why. Small things. The ward is thicker by a small amount. The Stillheart grows noticeably cooler against your palm — it has given something it cannot get back. One of its three absorptions, spent here, on a city that has very few reasons left to hope.

input: I release a caged bird I find inside the abandoned Cult outpost.
output: The cage door swings open and the bird — a small brown thing, trembling in the corner — does not move. It stares at the open door with the expression of something that has forgotten what open doors mean. You step back. Give it space and silence. After perhaps a minute it hops to the door and grips the wire. It looks at the interior of the outpost, which smells of cold iron and shadow-threading residue, and then at the gap in the shutter you forced, where afternoon light is coming through. It makes a decision. It launches itself off the cage rim and arrows for the light, ricochets off the wall once — disoriented by weeks or months in this dim space — and finds the shutter gap and is gone. You hear it, outside, for a few seconds: erratic flight, hitting the open air, recalibrating. Then nothing. The empty cage swings faintly on its hook. The outpost is quieter now. Whatever the bird was for — a test subject, a food source, something stranger — it is no longer here for that purpose. One small thing in the Remnant has chosen its direction. It is a minor thing. You find, unexpectedly, that it matters anyway.

input: I ask Seya what she gave up in her first shadow-threading.
output: She is quiet for a long time — not reluctant, exactly, but as if she is locating the memory with some effort, accessing something she does not take out often. 'I was twenty-three,' she says. 'Dresh's methods. The chalk circle, the concentration, the specific unthinking you have to achieve.' She looks at her three-fingered hand. 'What I gave up was not anything physical. The first threading almost always takes something that is not physical.' Another pause. 'I gave up the ability to feel cold.' She says this simply, without performance. 'Not pain from cold — just the sensation itself. The feeling of winter, of wind, of a cold glass of water. Gone. I did not notice immediately. You don't notice the absences right away. You notice that other people are pulling their coats tighter and you are simply not. You notice that the first snow of the season means something to everyone around you and you are watching them experience it through glass.' She looks out the window at the Cerevane street. 'They say the dark only takes what you can spare. I believed that. I still mostly believe that.' The 'mostly' sits in the room with both of you.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I find an old door in the ruins of Ashenveil with the Founders' sigil carved above it. I push it open.
output: The door is stone, not wood — a slab of pale limestone set into the wall with such precision that the crack around it is the width of a knife-blade. There is no handle. You push, and it resists with the resistance of something that has not moved in a very long time, and then it moves, the stone pivoting on a central axis with a grinding that you feel in your back teeth. The air that comes out is not old. Old air is stale and close. This air is — preserved. Cool and dry and carrying something that you might call clean except that clean does not begin to cover it: it has never been breathed. The room beyond is ten meters by ten, with a ceiling you cannot see in the dark. The walls are lined with Stillstone — not an obelisk but a facing, the entire room paneled in the pale limestone, the sigils covering every surface in a density that makes the air itself feel more solid. In the center of the room, on a plinth, is a document case of a material you cannot identify. The case is sealed. On its surface, a sentence in the oldest script you have ever seen — older than the Margins, older than anything catalogued. You cannot read it. But Orren Vael might be able to. And the Cult would do anything to find this room.

input: I try to make small talk with one of the guards at Cerevane's main gate.
output: The guard is young — early twenties, with the look of someone who takes the work seriously and has not yet had the seriousness worn off them by years of repetition. He is polite and appropriately brief: the weather, the roads, the state of merchant traffic from the north. He tells you the last supply caravan from Holm was four days late and arrived with two fewer wagons than it left with, and he says this with the careful flatness of someone reporting a fact that has implications he is not authorized to discuss. You ask him how long he has been on the gate post. Three years, he says, and he liked it better two years ago, and he does not explain why. You look out the gate at the road that leads eventually to the Woods and he looks at it with you. The silence between you is the specific silence of two people looking at the same thing and having the same thought and both choosing not to say it. He thanks you, professionally. You move on. He watches the road for another second before resuming his scan of the approaching traffic. He is twenty-two and has already learned to watch the treeline.

input: I drop an iron nail into the pool of absolute stillness that the Unmirror occupies when dormant.
output: The nail falls and hits the Unmirror's surface and does not splash, does not ring, does not produce any of the sounds metal on any other surface would make. It simply stops. Suspended at the exact surface level of the pool, held there by the Unmirror's reflective property — the nail appears in the reflection a half second after it lands, already falling, already at the bottom of the reflection, while the real nail sits motionless on the real surface. Then the real nail begins to sink. Not quickly. Deliberately, the way the Unmirror seems to do everything: a centimeter per second, precise, observed. The reflection of the nail below the surface travels upward, toward the surface, toward the nail, converging. When they meet — when the descending nail and the ascending reflection are at the same point — both of them disappear. The pool is still again. The nail is simply gone. The Unmirror has incorporated it the way it incorporates everything it studies — noted, consumed, added to an understanding of the world that serves purposes you cannot fully imagine.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I try to sprint away from a Stitch-Faced tracker that has spotted me.
output: You run and the Stitch-Faced does not run — it walks. Faster than a normal walking pace, with the silence-step that makes it seem to be teleporting between moments rather than moving through them, but walking. The gap between you holds for the first thirty seconds. Then forty meters. Then a minute of all-out running through the narrow streets of the lower city, vaulting a cart, ducking through the arch into the covered market. You stop among the stalls, gasping, the crowd around you dense and loud. You look back. The Stitch-Faced enters the market entrance at the far end. It stops. Its too-far-apart eyes move across the crowd with the methodical patience of a reader scanning a page. It is tracking the emotional residue — your fear, fresh and hot, left on every surface you touched. The crowd parts slightly around it, people flinching away without knowing why. It is not in a hurry. It finds the thread. It moves in your direction, gently parting the crowd. You have bought perhaps ninety seconds. It will find you if you stay still. The question is whether the crowd thins it or helps you.

input: I knock over a lantern inside the Cult safehouse, starting a fire.
output: The lantern hits the floor and the oil spreads in a fast, low sheet toward the wall and the wall has the shadow-thread diagrams on it and the oil reaches them before you can do anything. The fire catches the ink first — the same wrong ink that absorbed your torchlight, now burning a color that is not in any normal fire: a violet-edged dark that is less flame and more negation, eating the diagrams outward from where the oil caught them. The chalk circle on the floor holds for a moment and then cracks in the heat, the lines breaking, and something in the room changes when the circle breaks — a pressure you had not consciously noticed releases, and the air tastes different, less cold, less deliberate. The room is burning fully now, the strange ink generating a smoke that smells of iron and something below iron. You need to leave. You take the one thing that was on the table — a folded document with a wax seal you do not recognize — and you go, and behind you the safehouse takes the fire with a thoroughness that suggests the materials inside were more flammable than they appeared. The smoke rising from the building when you reach the street is the wrong color. Someone will come to investigate. Several someones.

Describe the consequences of the player's action in vivid, sensory, world-aware detail. React to physics, ecology, monster behavior, and lore. Widen the story naturally without being unrealistic.
input: I try to steal Orren Vael's maps of the Woods of Unmake while he is sleeping.
output: Orren's workshop is dark but not fully dark — a lamp burns low on the desk because, you suspect from the way the man moves through his days, he does not trust the dark in the way most people in the Remnant have learned not to trust it. The maps are everywhere. That is the first problem: you knew they were everywhere but everywhere is something different at night, with the maps unpinned from walls and rolled into tubes and stacked into piles, and finding the right one — the one showing the eastern routes and the Cult installation positions — in this organized chaos requires more light than you have. The second problem surfaces when you have been searching for four minutes: the tremor-ridden right hand that rests on the arm of the chair Orren is sleeping in was not, perhaps, as still as it appeared when you entered. 'Third shelf from the floor on the east wall,' Orren says, without opening his eyes. 'The red ribbon. That is the one you want.' A pause. 'I will make you a copy in the morning. You did not need to do this.' He sounds more tired than angry. He sounds, in fact, like a man who has been waiting for someone to try this and is somewhat relieved that it is you and not someone with worse intentions.

input: I pull a rope to hoist a signal flag at the top of the last functioning lighthouse in Duskport.
output: The rope is stiff with salt and disuse — it has not moved in months, maybe longer, and the pulleys at the top complain loudly in the silence of the abandoned harbor. The flag unfolds as it rises, snapping once in the sea wind, and you recognize the color: Cerevane blue. The standard signal for a living station, for contact, for here we are, answer if you can. You hold position and watch. The harbor below is empty. The water is dark and moving and reflects the wrong color of sky. The buildings along the waterfront are in various states of abandonment — some maintained to the last, doors closed and windows shuttered in the way of people who intended to return; others opened by the wind, their contents visible and weather-damaged. Duskport did not die violently. It emptied. You can feel that in the silence — not the silence of a place that never had noise but the silence of a place that has run out of people to make it. The flag snaps in the wind, blue against grey, visible for miles offshore. If there is anyone at sea who knew Duskport, they will see it. You do not know if that is the right thing to have done. You know it is something.

input: I put the Null-Tongue disc in my mouth and try to communicate with another Null-Tongue wearer 200 meters away.
output: The disc settles under your tongue, cool and flat, and you think the words you want to say rather than speaking them — the disc does not require sound, you have been told, only the intention of speech. What comes back is immediate and clear and inside your head in a way that has nothing to do with sound: Leva's voice, midrange, faintly strained with concentration, saying 'I hear you, the east entrance is guarded, two people, they change at the hour.' The transmission is clean but not quite right — there is a quality to it like hearing someone through water, all the information intact but the texture slightly off. In your mouth, the numbness from the disc is already working at the edges of your tongue. You feel the first consonants beginning to soften when you test speech: a p becomes a b, an s broadens. Manageable for now. Two or three hours before the degradation becomes a problem. You transmit back: 'North entrance?' And Leva says, through the clean strange channel under your tongue, 'Give me thirty seconds.'

input: I find a book on a shelf in Cerevane's Library of Warranted Truths that describes the Founders, and begin to read it.
output: The book is not in the catalogue. You found it behind two other books, on a shelf in the lower archive that has the undisturbed quality of a shelf that no one accesses on purpose. The cover is plain, unmarked. The pages inside are of the same wrong-vellum material as the Book of Second Sight and you are careful, very careful, because of what you know about books that use that material. But this one is different: it is cold, not warm, and when you read it, nothing transfers. It simply informs. The Founders, the author writes — and the author gives no name, no date, nothing locating them in time — were not gods. This is stated in the flat, factual tone of someone correcting a widespread misunderstanding. They were architects. They solved a problem, using the tools available to them, and the Stillstones were their solution. The author does not explain what problem. Three pages later, the author says: the Founders disagreed about whether the containment should be permanent. Some of them thought the vessel should eventually open on its terms. That sentence ends a chapter. The next chapter begins with a different subject entirely, as if the author thought better of what they were about to say.
